stem: How many distinct labeled trees can be formed on \( [[n]] \) labeled vertices? (Equivalently, how many spanning trees does \( K_{[[n]]} \) have?)
solution: By Cayley's formula, the number of labeled trees on \( n \) vertices is \( n^{n-2} \). Here that is \( [[n]]^{[[n]]-2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the exponent \( n-1 \) instead of \( n-2 \).
This uses the exponent \( n-3 \) instead of \( n-2 \).
This uses base \( n-1 \) instead of \( n \).
This counts permutations \( (n-1)! \), a common wrong closed form.
This counts all permutations \( n! \) of the vertices.
This counts the edges of \( K_n \), not its spanning trees.
This is off by one from the correct count.

stem: How many distinct ROOTED labeled trees are there on \( [[n]] \) labeled vertices (i.e., a labeled tree together with a choice of one vertex as the root)?
solution: By Cayley's formula there are \( n^{n-2} \) unrooted labeled trees on \( n \) vertices, and each can be rooted at any of its \( n \) vertices, giving \( n \cdot n^{n-2} = n^{n-1} \) rooted labeled trees. Here that is \( [[n]]^{[[n]]-1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the unrooted count \( n^{n-2} \); it forgets to multiply by the \( n \) choices of root.
This uses the exponent \( n \) instead of \( n-1 \).
This uses base \( n-1 \) instead of \( n \).
This multiplies the unrooted-tree exponent pattern incorrectly.
This counts permutations \( (n-1)! \), a common wrong closed form.
This is off by one from the correct count.

stem: Consider all labeled forests on vertex set \( \{1, 2, \ldots, [[n]]\} \) that consist of exactly \( [[k]] \) trees, where vertices \( 1, 2, \ldots, [[k]] \) are required to be the roots of these \( [[k]] \) trees (one root per tree). How many such labeled forests are there?
solution: This is the generalized Cayley forest formula: the number of labeled forests on \( n \) vertices with \( k \) trees rooted at \( k \) specified vertices is \( k \cdot n^{n-k-1} \). Here that is \( [[k]] \cdot [[n]]^{[[n]]-[[k]]-1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the factor of \( k \) out front.
This uses the exponent \( n-k \) instead of \( n-k-1 \).
This keeps the plain Cayley exponent \( n-2 \) instead of adjusting it for \( k \) roots.
This is the ordinary (unrooted, single-tree) Cayley formula, ignoring the forest structure entirely.
This confuses the forest count with a simple choice of which vertices are roots.
This is off by one from the correct count.
